name: Child's Pose
instructions: Kneel on the floor with your big toes touching and knees hip-width apart. Slowly lower your torso between your thighs and extend your arms forward, resting your palms on the floor. Hold the position while breathing deeply, feeling the stretch along your spine and lats. Relax your neck and let your forehead rest on the mat.
feelRight: You should feel a gentle, soothing stretch through your lower back, lats, and shoulders. The pose should feel grounding and relaxing as you breathe into the back body.
feelWrong: If your hips lift off your heels or your lower back feels strained, your knees may be too close together. Avoid overarching your neck by keeping your forehead down and shoulders relaxed.
notes: Place a pillow under your thighs for extra support if your knees are sensitive. Use this pose anytime between intense sets to reset your breath.

name: Standing Hamstring Stretch
instructions: Stand tall and extend one leg forward with your heel on the floor and toes pointing up. Hinge at your hips to slowly lean forward, keeping your back flat, until you feel a stretch along the back of your extended leg. Hold the position and breathe, then switch legs. Keep a slight bend in your standing knee to protect the joint.
feelRight: You should feel a pulling sensation along the back of your extended thigh without sharp pain. The stretch should deepen as you exhale and relax into the hinge.
feelWrong: If you round your lower back or lock your standing knee, you shift tension into your spine instead of the hamstring. Bouncing or jerking into the stretch can trigger a muscle strain.
notes: Keep your hips square and facing forward. If you cannot reach your toes comfortably, rest your hands on your thigh or a block.

name: Standing Quad Stretch
instructions: Stand tall and shift your weight onto one leg. Bend your opposite knee, bringing your heel toward your glute, and grasp your ankle with your hand. Pull your heel gently toward your body until you feel a stretch in the front of your thigh. Keep your knees close together and engage your core to avoid arching your lower back.
feelRight: You should feel a steady stretch along the front of your thigh without pinching in your hip. The standing leg should feel stable and grounded.
feelWrong: If you yank your heel hard or arch your lower back, you risk straining your hip flexor or quad. Letting your knee drift outward reduces the stretch and stresses the knee joint.
notes: Hold onto a wall or chair for balance if needed. Avoid pressing your heel directly into your glute — stop when you feel a comfortable stretch.

name: Pigeon Pose
instructions: Start in a tabletop position on your hands and knees. Bring your right knee forward and place it behind your right wrist, then angle your right shin slightly diagonal to the left. Extend your left leg straight back with your toes pointed, keeping your hips square. Slowly lower your torso over your front leg, resting on your forearms or forehead, and breathe.
feelRight: You should feel a deep, external rotation stretch in the front hip and glute. The sensation should be strong but not sharp, releasing as you relax into the pose.
feelWrong: If your front ankle is tucked too close to your hip or your hips are tilted to one side, you may feel knee pain instead of a hip stretch. Dropping all your weight into the front knee without supporting with your arms can overstress the joint.
notes: Slide a folded towel under your front hip if it does not rest comfortably on the floor. Keep your back foot flexed to protect the ankle and knee.

name: Lat Stretch (Doorway)
instructions: Stand in a doorway and raise both arms overhead, placing your palms on the door frame slightly wider than shoulder width. Lean your torso forward through the doorway until you feel a stretch along the sides of your back and armpits. Keep your core braced and your chest open as you hold the position. Breathe deeply to allow the lats to release.
feelRight: You should feel a broad stretch running from your armpits down the sides of your ribcage. The sensation should open up your shoulders without pinching.
feelWrong: If you shrug your shoulders up toward your ears or let your lower back arch, the stretch shifts out of your lats. Leaning too aggressively can strain the shoulder capsule.
notes: Adjust your hand height on the frame to target different parts of the lat. For a deeper stretch, bend your knees slightly and hinge at the hips.

name: Neck Release
instructions: Sit or stand with a tall spine and relaxed shoulders. Gently tilt your head to one side, bringing your ear toward your shoulder without lifting the opposite shoulder. Place the same-side hand on your head and apply very light pressure to deepen the stretch. Hold for several breaths, then repeat on the other side and with a chin-tuck variation.
feelRight: You should feel a gentle elongation along the side of your neck, free of any sharp or pinching sensations. The stretch should feel like a release of tension rather than a pull.
feelWrong: If you yank your head with your hand or roll your head in full circles, you can irritate the cervical spine. Raising your shoulder toward your ear as you tilt negates the stretch and creates unnecessary tension.
notes: Keep your jaw soft and your teeth slightly apart to avoid clenching. For a deeper release, add a gentle chin tuck before tilting your head.

name: Standing Forward Fold
instructions: Stand with your feet hip-width apart and a micro-bend in your knees. Hinge at your hips to fold your torso forward, letting your arms hang heavy toward the floor. Relax your head and neck completely, and hold opposite elbows with your hands to sway gently. Breathe deeply into your lower back and hamstrings.
feelRight: You should feel a lengthening sensation through your hamstrings and lower back as gravity does the work. The fold should feel releasing and passive rather than forced.
feelWrong: If you lock your knees straight or round your spine aggressively, you place excessive strain on the lower back and sciatic nerve. Forcing your hands to the floor at the expense of good form reduces the stretch benefit.
notes: Bend your knees more if you feel any sharp pulling in your hamstrings. Place your hands on blocks or a bench if they do not reach the floor comfortably.

name: Seated Spinal Twist
instructions: Sit on the floor with both legs extended in front of you. Bend your right knee and step your right foot over your left leg, placing it flat on the floor outside your left knee. Twist your torso to the right, hooking your left elbow outside your right knee, and look over your right shoulder. Breathe deeply and lengthen your spine on each inhale, twisting deeper on each exhale.
feelRight: You should feel a gentle rotation through your thoracic spine and a stretch in your glute and hip. The twist should feel freeing and should never cause sharp back pain.
feelWrong: If you collapse your chest or hunch your shoulders forward, you compress the spine instead of lengthening through the twist. Forcing the rotation from your lower back rather than your torso can strain the lumbar discs.
notes: Sit on a folded blanket if your hips are tight and your sit bones do not reach the floor. Keep your opposite hand pressed into the floor behind you for support.

name: Figure-Four Glute Stretch
instructions: Lie on your back with both knees bent and feet flat on the floor. Cross your right ankle over your left thigh, just above the knee, creating a figure-four shape. Reach your hands through your legs and clasp the back of your left thigh, gently pulling it toward your chest. Hold the position and breathe, then switch sides.
feelRight: You should feel a deep stretch in the glute and outer hip of the crossed leg. The sensation should be a comfortable release with no sharp pain in the knee or hip.
feelWrong: If you feel pressure on the outside of the bent knee or in the hip joint, your ankle may be placed too high on the thigh. Pulling the bottom leg too aggressively can overstretch the hamstring attachment.
notes: Keep your head and shoulders relaxed on the floor to avoid neck tension. For a deeper stretch, press the crossed knee gently away from your body with your elbow.

name: Downward Dog Calf Stretch
instructions: Start in a tabletop position, then tuck your toes and lift your hips up and back into a downward dog shape. Press your chest toward your thighs and drive your heels alternately toward the floor, pedaling gently. Keep a slight bend in your knees and focus on lengthening your spine. Hold still on the final side and breathe into the stretch.
feelRight: You should feel a lengthening pull through your calves and Achilles tendons as you press your heels down. The stretch should also open your shoulders and hamstrings.
feelWrong: If you lock your knees or force your heels flat too aggressively, you overload the gastrocnemius and risk a calf strain. Rounding your upper back excessively shifts the work out of your calves and into your shoulders.
notes: Pedal your heels gently at first before settling into a static hold on one side. Keep your arms actively pressing into the floor to distribute weight evenly.

name: Kneeling Hip Flexor Stretch
instructions: Start in a half-kneeling position with your right knee on the floor and your left foot flat in front of you, both knees bent at 90 degrees. Engage your core and tuck your pelvis slightly under (posterior pelvic tilt). Gently lean forward into your left leg while keeping your torso upright and your right glute squeezed until you feel a stretch in the front of your right hip.
feelRight: You should feel a moderate, pulling stretch in the front of the hip and upper thigh of the kneeling leg. The core should be active to maintain the pelvic tilt.
feelWrong: Arching your lower back aggressively shifts the stretch away from the hip flexor and compresses the lumbar spine. Leaning too far forward without engaging the glute reduces the effectiveness of the stretch.
notes: To deepen the stretch, reach the arm on the kneeling side up toward the ceiling and slightly over to the opposite side. Place a pad or folded mat under the kneeling knee for comfort.
